players.name: Yehvann Diouf
players.bio: The Nice stopper inherited the gloves from Marcin Bulka and made them his own. Still early in his international career with just 2 caps, Diouf arrives at his first World Cup as Senegal's undisputed No.1.
players.facts: His predecessor Bulka was voted best GK in Ligue 1 — Diouf had some serious shoes (and gloves) to fill.

players.name: Mamadou Sarr
players.bio: Just 20 years old and already a Chelsea regular, Mamadou Sarr is one of European football's most exciting defensive prospects. Composed, technically sharp and mature beyond his years, he brings elite-club quality to Senegal's backline.
players.facts: Born in 2005, Sarr is young enough that Senegal's 2002 World Cup final run happened before he could walk.

players.name: Kalidou Koulibaly
players.bio: The Lion King of Senegal's defence. With over 100 caps and stints at Napoli and Chelsea, Koulibaly is one of the finest centre-backs of his generation. Now at Al-Hilal, the veteran leader brings experience money can't buy.
players.facts: Koulibaly spent nine years at Napoli, becoming such a legend that fans still sing his name at the Diego Armando Maradona Stadium.

players.name: Abdoulaye Seck
players.bio: A seasoned campaigner with 22 caps and a surprisingly handy four international goals for a centre-back. Seck has journeyed across Europe and the Middle East, and his no-nonsense defending makes him a reliable squad option at 34.
players.facts: Abdoulaye Seck has scored 4 international goals — more than several of Senegal's outfield players. Defenders who can finish: a rare breed.

players.name: Idrissa Gueye
players.bio: With 131 caps, 'Gana' is one of Senegal's most-capped players ever. The tireless, ball-winning midfielder terrorised Premier League midfields at Everton and PSG. A warrior in the engine room and a true Lions legend.
players.facts: Gueye is so good at winning the ball back that Pep Guardiola once singled him out as one of the hardest midfielders to play against.

players.name: Pathé Ciss
players.bio: A combative, hardworking midfielder who has made Rayo Vallecano's feisty midfield his home. Pathé Ciss brings grit and discipline to Senegal's engine room, using his 30 caps to prove he belongs at the highest level.
players.facts: Pathé Ciss plays for Rayo Vallecano — a club famous for their left-wing fan politics. The midfielder fits right into the fighting spirit.

players.name: Assane Diao
players.bio: A teenage sensation with blistering pace and dribbling ability, Diao burst onto the Serie A scene with Como after shining in La Liga. Still only 20, this could be his World Cup coming-out party on the grandest stage.
players.facts: Diao was born in Pamplona, Spain, making him one of several Senegal internationals raised in Europe who chose the Lions of Teranga.

players.name: Lamine Camara
players.bio: One of African football's most exciting midfielders, Camara is a box-to-box dynamo with Monaco who chips in with crucial goals. Seven international goals in 33 caps at just 22 is a remarkable return for a midfielder.
players.facts: Camara shares his birthday — January 1st — making him either very easy to remember or a nightmare for party planning every New Year.

players.name: Bamba Dieng
players.bio: The pacy striker made waves with Marseille and has kept his international place despite a move to Lorient. Known for his directness and clinical finishing, Dieng offers Senegal a lively option off the bench or from the start.
players.facts: Bamba Dieng famously scored a hat-trick for Marseille in a single Ligue 1 match — not bad for someone who almost missed out on the 2022 World Cup squad.

players.name: Sadio Mané
players.bio: Africa's greatest? The argument is hard to win against Mané. A Premier League title, Champions League, and Africa Cup of Nations winner, the No.10 remains Senegal's heartbeat with 55 goals in 128 caps. Pure royalty.
players.facts: Mané grew up so poor he couldn't afford a football — he and friends used rags. He now earns a reported €40m a year at Al-Nassr.

players.name: Nicolas Jackson
players.bio: The Chelsea graduate turned Bayern Munich striker brings pace, power and a sharp eye for goal. Jackson has grown enormously since his Chelsea days and arrives in 2026 as one of Senegal's most potent attacking threats.
players.facts: Jackson became Bayern Munich's first Senegalese player — quite the stamp of arrival for a striker still only in his mid-twenties.

players.name: Cherif Ndiaye
players.bio: A determined forward who has carved out a steady international career with 19 caps and four goals. Cherif Ndiaye's journey through European football — including Turkish top-flight football with Samsunspor — shows real resilience.
players.facts: Ndiaye plays in Turkey, joining a surprisingly long list of Senegalese players who've made the Super Lig their home.

players.name: Iliman Ndiaye
players.bio: Born in Rouen but raised in Senegalese football culture, Iliman Ndiaye is a clever, creative attacker who dazzled at Sheffield United before Everton came calling. His ability to unlock defences makes him a fan favourite.
players.facts: Ndiaye famously helped Sheffield United win promotion to the Premier League — the Blades' faithful still hold him in the highest regard.

players.name: Ismail Jakobs
players.bio: Born in Cologne, Germany, Jakobs chose Senegal over Die Mannschaft and has never looked back. The attacking left-back brings pace and width from his flank, earning 30 caps and becoming a key part of Senegal's system.
players.facts: Jakobs is German-born and could have played for Germany — instead he chose Senegal, much to the delight of the Lions of Teranga faithful.

players.name: Krépin Diatta
players.bio: Versatile and quick, Diatta can operate as a winger or right-back, making him a manager's dream option. With 61 caps, he's one of Senegal's more experienced squad members and knows what it takes at tournament level.
players.facts: Diatta suffered a serious knee injury in 2021 that threatened his career — he recovered to become one of Monaco's most reliable performers.

players.name: Édouard Mendy
players.bio: The Champions League winner with Chelsea in 2021 is a commanding presence between the sticks. Mendy's shot-stopping ability and aerial dominance made him one of Europe's elite goalkeepers. Now starring in Saudi Arabia's Pro League.
players.facts: Mendy was working in supermarkets and rejected by clubs before getting his break at 28 — proof that it's never too late to make it.

players.name: Pape Matar Sarr
players.bio: A dynamic, energetic midfielder who burst onto the scene at Tottenham, Pape Matar Sarr combines tenacity with technical quality. At just 23 with 40 caps already, he's a cornerstone of Senegal's midfield future.
players.facts: Sarr made his Senegal debut as a teenager and has racked up 40 caps before most players even get their first call-up. Frighteningly good.

players.name: Ismaïla Sarr
players.bio: Electrifying, direct and deceptively strong, Ismaïla Sarr has been lighting up English football for years. With 19 goals in 83 caps, the Crystal Palace winger is Senegal's chief creative threat from wide areas.
players.facts: Sarr's sprint speed has been clocked among the fastest in the Premier League — defenders who face him often need a lie-down afterwards.

players.name: Moussa Niakhaté
players.bio: A tall, composed centre-back who has been a cornerstone of Lyon's defence, Niakhaté reads the game exceptionally well. With 31 caps, he's established himself as one of Senegal's most dependable defensive options.
players.facts: Niakhaté has Bundesliga experience with Mainz 05 before moving to Lyon, giving him one of the more well-travelled CVs in the squad.

players.name: Ibrahim Mbaye
players.bio: At just 18, Ibrahim Mbaye is the tournament's youngest Senegalese player and perhaps the most exciting. Already a PSG academy product with 3 international goals in 11 caps, his World Cup debut could be the start of something special.
players.facts: Born in 2008, Mbaye was six years old when Sadio Mané scored his first World Cup goal. Now they're teammates. Football is wild.

players.name: Habib Diarra
players.bio: A box-to-box midfielder with a knack for arriving late into the penalty area, Diarra has impressed at Sunderland with his energy and goals. Four international goals in 21 caps suggest a player with plenty more to give.
players.facts: Diarra plays in the Championship with Sunderland — making him one of the few World Cup players plying his trade outside the top flight.

players.name: Bara Sapoko Ndiaye
players.bio: The youngest player in Senegal's 2026 squad, Ndiaye is a Bayern Munich starlet who turned 18 just before the tournament. With only one cap to his name, this World Cup could be the beginning of an extraordinary story.
players.facts: Born on 31 December 2007, Ndiaye is the squad's New Year's Eve baby — and Bayern Munich's next big midfield hope.

players.name: Mory Diaw
players.bio: The dependable third-choice goalkeeper at Le Havre, Mory Diaw brings Ligue 1 experience and calm professionalism to Senegal's goalkeeping ranks. Five caps and a trustworthy pair of hands make him an asset to any squad.
players.facts: Diaw plays for Le Havre — one of France's oldest clubs, founded in 1872. He's keeping history between the sticks every week.

players.name: Antoine Mendy
players.bio: Born in Marseille, the young Nice centre-back has opted for Senegal at international level, earning 7 caps by age 22. Composed and powerful, Mendy is one of Ligue 1's most promising defensive talents.
players.facts: Mendy is a Marseille-born defender who plays for city rivals Nice — a transfer decision that requires a thick skin in the south of France.

players.name: El Hadji Malick Diouf
players.bio: The exciting young left-back from Dakar has made West Ham his home and already amassed 20 caps at just 21. Bombing forward with pace and quality, El Hadji Malick Diouf is a modern fullback tailor-made for the big stage.
players.facts: Named El Hadji — like Senegalese legend El Hadji Diouf — but no relation. He's building his own legacy, thank you very much.

players.name: Pape Gueye
players.bio: Born in Montreuil, France, Pape Gueye is a commanding central midfielder who has become a key figure at Villarreal. With 41 caps and 5 international goals, he adds experience, mobility and a mean long-range shot.
players.facts: Gueye once completed a move to Marseille before it was dramatically cancelled — he ended up at Villarreal instead. Everything happens for a reason.
